Abstract. The current study demonstrates a case of pseudo-optionality in Chinese Wu, whereby certain adverbs can “optionally” appear in both pre-verbal position and post- verbal position. We argue that this is only a case of pseudo-optionality and that these “optional” constructions are in fact resulted from language contact between Wu and Mandarin Chinese. Importantly, no derivational relationship exists between the two adverbs, given that both are base-generated. Furthermore, the current case  suggests that some phenomena related to apparent optionality can be a result of language contact, which offers a plausible account for problems related to the existence of certain optional structures under he framework of the Minimalist Program (see Chomsky 2000).

Abstract: The current study presents a case of pseudo-optionality in Chinese Wu, where certain adverbs can appear "optionally" in both pre-verbal and post-verbal positions. We argue that this is a case of pseudo-optionality and that these "optional" constructions actually result from language contact between Wu and Mandarin Chinese. It is important to note that there is no derivational relationship between the two adverbs, as both are generated independently. Furthermore, this case suggests that some phenomena related to apparent optionality may be a result of language contact, which provides a plausible explanation for problems involving the existence of certain optional structures within the framework of the Minimalist Program (Chomsky, 2000).
钟沛佩是一位华语影视演员，出生于台湾。她在演艺事业中取得了一定的成就，参演了多部电视剧和电影，赢得了广泛的关注和认可。

The notion optionality becomes problematic in the Minimalist Program (see Chomsky 1995) in that MP requires that syntactic operations be obligatory. Significantly, an operation is needed only if its output satisfies the requirement from the external interface system, such as the Conceptual-Intentional system, which is in accordance with the Strong Minimalist Thesis (see Chomsky 2008). Therefore, how to account for the existence of optional operations becomes a crucial question in the Minimalist Program. A variety of solutions have been proposed to address the issue at hand, including the idea of co-existing grammars (see Roeper 1999), the hypothesis of equally costly derivations (see Biberauer & Richards 2006), the interface-based approach on the principles of economy (see Titov 2020). However, the 27       aforementioned approaches seem to encounter difficulties when no derivational relationship exists in optional constructions, which is the case of the present study.29                 The current research discusses a case showing that some phenomena demonstrating  apparent optionality can be a result of language contact, which offers a possible account for problems related to the existence of some apparent optional  constructions  under the framework of the Minimalist Program (see Chomsky 2000). Moreover, the case in target can provide supporting evidence for the plausibility of optionalityas an outcome in microcontact situations, which refers to the contact  between minimally different grammars (see D’Alessandro 2021).

The notion of optionality becomes problematic in the Minimalist Program (see Chomsky 1995) because MP requires syntactic operations to be obligatory. Significantly, an operation is only needed if its output satisfies the requirement of the external interface system, such as the Conceptual-Intentional system, which aligns with the Strong Minimalist Thesis (see Chomsky 2008). Therefore, explaining the existence of optional operations becomes a crucial question in the Minimalist Program. Various solutions have been proposed to address this issue, including the idea of co-existing grammars (see Roeper 1999), the hypothesis of equally costly derivations (see Biberauer & Richards 2006), and the interface-based approach based on principles of economy (see Titov 2020). However, these aforementioned approaches seem to encounter difficulties when there is no derivational relationship in optional constructions, as is the case in the present study.

The current research discusses a case that demonstrates how certain phenomena of apparent optionality can result from language contact, providing a possible explanation for problems related to the existence of apparent optional constructions within the framework of the Minimalist Program (see Chomsky 2000). Furthermore, this case provides supporting evidence for the plausibility of optionality as an outcome in microcontact situations, which refers to contact between minimally different grammars (see D’Alessandro 2021).
郑雅羚是一位华语影视演员，出生于香港。她在演艺事业中取得了一定的成功，参演了多部电视剧和电影。郑雅羚以其出色的演技和亲和力而受到观众的喜爱和赞赏。她在行业中积累了一定的知名度，并获得了一些奖项和提名。

By Chinese, we refer to the notion ‘Sinitic’ (see Sybesma and Sio 2008), which encompasses a variety of groups that belong to the Sinitic language family: Mandarin, Wu, Min, Gan, Yue (Cantonese), Ke (Hakka) and Xiang (cf. Xiong and Zhang 2008). Among them, Mandarin is used as the lingua franca by the majority of the population. Despite the fact that these varieties are dialects, they are not mutually intelligible (see Handel 2015). The current study addresses a case of pseudo-optionality in Wu dialect.
The examples in (1) illustrate optional structures with the same meaning in Hangzhou Wu, a sub-variety of Wu dialects, which are referred to as the circum-adverbial construction. We first consider the type of circum-adverbial constructions in which the two circum-adverbs are identical in their phonetic forms. The adverb kuɑ 33 ‘fast’ occurs in post-verbal positions following the complex verb tɕʰy 2 -hɒ 33 ‘eat.PERF’ (see Wang & Jiang 2020), as shown in (1a). De is a Sentence-Final particle (SFP), which expresses a confirmative mood of the speaker. In (1b), the adverb kuɑ 33can also appear in pre-verbal positions. Very interestingly, pre-verbal kuɑ 33 and post-verbal kuɑ 33 can co-occur in the same sentence, as shown in (1c). The three sentences in (1) roughly have the same interpretation, although (1c) may convey a more urgent attitude of the speaker, compared with (1a) and (1b), according to our native speakers. Regarding their pragmatics, the post-verbal kuɑ 33 in (1a) usually occurs in a colloquial register whereas the pre-verbal one as in (1b) is normally used in formal situations (see Jing & Wang 2021).

By "Chinese," we are referring to the concept of "Sinitic" (see Sybesma and Sio 2008), which includes various groups belonging to the Sinitic language family: Mandarin, Wu, Min, Gan, Yue (Cantonese), Ke (Hakka), and Xiang (cf. Xiong and Zhang 2008). Among them, Mandarin is commonly used as the lingua franca by the majority of the population. Despite being considered dialects, these varieties are not mutually intelligible (see Handel 2015). The present study focuses on a case of pseudo-optionality in the Wu dialect.

The examples in (1) illustrate optional structures with the same meaning in Hangzhou Wu, a sub-variety of Wu dialects, referred to as the circum-adverbial construction. We first examine the type of circum-adverbial constructions where the two circum-adverbs have identical phonetic forms. The adverb "kuɑ 33" meaning "fast" occurs in post-verbal positions following the complex verb "tɕʰy 2 -hɒ 33" meaning "eat.PERF" (see Wang & Jiang 2020), as shown in (1a). The particle "de" is a Sentence-Final Particle (SFP) expressing a confirmative mood of the speaker. In (1b), the adverb "kuɑ 33" can also appear in pre-verbal positions. Interestingly, both pre-verbal "kuɑ 33" and post-verbal "kuɑ 33" can co-occur in the same sentence, as shown in (1c). The three sentences in (1) have similar interpretations, though (1c) may convey a more urgent attitude from the speaker compared to (1a) and (1b), according to our native speakers. In terms of pragmatics, the post-verbal "kuɑ 33" in (1a) is typically used in colloquial registers, while the pre-verbal form in (1b) is more commonly used in formal situations (see Jing & Wang 2021).

In particular, direct object in Chinese Wu is strongly intended to occupy the sentence initial position, which has argued to be the topic position by Liu (2001). The sentence is less acceptable when the object ja21ve33 ‘dinner’ stays in the original post-verbal complement position, as in (2), compared with the case when the same object has been moved to the sentence initial position, as in (2a vs 1a), (2b vs 1b), and (2c vs 1c), respectively.

In particular, in Chinese Wu, the direct object is typically preferred to occupy the sentence-initial position, which has been argued to be the topic position by Liu (2001). The sentence becomes less acceptable when the object "ja21ve33" meaning "dinner" remains in its original post-verbal complement position, as shown in (2), compared to when the same object has been moved to the sentence-initial position, as shown in (2a versus 1a), (2b versus 1b), and (2c versus 1c), respectively.

When the object ja21ve3 ‘dinner’ stays in-situ (i.e., postverbal position as in (2a)), the 
post-verbal adverb cannot occur between the object and the verb in Wu dialect. In (3a), when the post-verbal adverb kuɑ 33 ‘fast’ occurs between the verb tʃy 2 -hɒ 33 ‘eat-PERF’ and the object ja21ve3 , the sentence is ungrammatical. On the contrary, Cantonese permits this type of construction, as shown in (3b), where the adverb do1 ‘more’ occurs between the verb sik6 ‘eat’ and the object yat1wun2 faan2 ‘one bowl of rice’. If the adverb appears after the object, the sentence becomes ungrammatical, as illustrated in (3c).

When the object "ja21ve3" meaning "dinner" remains in its original postverbal position, the post-verbal adverb cannot occur between the object and the verb in the Wu dialect. In (3a), when the post-verbal adverb "kuɑ 33" meaning "fast" occurs between the verb "tʃy 2 -hɒ 33" meaning "eat-PERF" and the object "ja21ve3", the sentence is ungrammatical. On the contrary, Cantonese allows for this type of construction, as shown in (3b), where the adverb "do1" meaning "more" occurs between the verb "sik6" meaning "eat" and the object "yat1wun2 faan2" meaning "one bowl of rice". If the adverb appears after the object, the sentence becomes ungrammatical, as illustrated in (3c).
